title: Roundabout and mirror
description: Roundabout and mirror: Has been reported before and response was that it is a private road. This is totally incorrect and when I sent an email in reponse to the interim highways manager giving dates etc of the road adoption I have been ignored. Current roadworks in place but when these are complete the roundabout needs repainting and the mirror on the post on Upper Brook Street opposite Lutton Close needs replacing. Too many near misses and minor accidents will lead to a major accident. People do not acknowledge the roundabout sign when travelling down Upper Brook Street and the road markings are very faded. The mirror is used as it is not possible to see traffic approaching on the left coming up Upper Brook Street.

detail: As per many complaints these issues have NOT been corrected and therefore are not CLOSED!!! Yes, the road requires urgent attention on Brynhafod lane, placing sandbags in large pot holes is not good enough to say job done. But the overflow of flood water every time we have adverse weather is due to the constant flood that has never been corrected in as much as 10 years. This gas caused the issues of today. Cars are finding it difficult to avoid the holes as they are far too many, that are far too deep.

description: Pothole / subsidence of footpath: I have reported this issue before! The footpath that runs from the play ground at Fitzwarine Drive, to the kissing gate between the memorial garden / Whittington Castle has started to sink / subside. The surface is badly cracked. The camber now makes it difficult to traverse in my wheelchair. This path is a public right of way and has streetlighting installed, so is used by many villagers, not just by myself. The grass on both sides of the path get very wet, especially in winter and the old moat channel gets water settling in it. If the camber on this path gets any worse I am afraid my chair will tilt and I could end up in the old moat. If this happens I would not be able to get out. This urgently needs to be looked at due to the danger aspect / risk of injury.

title: Removing a frequently overflowing bin
description:
Removing a frequently overflowing bin: I am writing to raise a concern regarding a street bin in my local area which is frequently overflowing and causing ongoing issues.

The bin is regularly filled beyond capacity, leading to litter, animal excretion spilling onto the surrounding pavement and street. This not only creates an unsightly environment but may also pose hygiene concerns and attract pests. Despite routine collection schedules, the problem persists and appears to be a recurring issue.

Please note that the council employees only empty the top bit of the bin and more than half stays there, we asked one of the staff who was collecting today that why is this not emptied fully, he replied his job is to just take the top bit which is overflowed. It smells very bad in the surroundings and it is at the entrance road of the grocery store.

Given the frequency of this problem, I would like to request that the council consider removing the bin from this location and place it somewhere more appropriate.

I would appreciate it if you could investigate this matter and advise on any actions that can be taken to resolve it.

Thank you for your time and attention. I look forward to your response.

title: Bins at Whittington Castle are overflowing.
detail:
Dear Shropshire Council, I am writing to report an ongoing issue with the waste bins located at Whittington Castle.

All three bins have been overflowing for well over a week, and as of today (Wednesday 6th May 2026), all three bins remain completely full, with rubbish spilling out onto the surrounding grounds. The situation appears to be worsening rather than improving.

This is becoming a significant concern, as the accumulation of waste poses a risk of attracting rats and other pests. Such an infestation could have serious consequences for the local environment, including harm to the ducks in the area, as well as risks to dogs that are regularly walked around the grounds. Additionally, this is a popular location for families, including young children and babies, and the current state of the area raises clear health and safety concerns.

I enclose some photos of all 3 bins for your attention.

I would appreciate it if this matter could be addressed as soon as possible, with the bins emptied and a more frequent collection schedule considered if necessary. Also I believe that having more bins located among the car park and around surrounding grounds will help keep on top of the ongoing litter issues.

Thank you for your attention to this issue. I look forward to your response.

Yours faithfully, Miss Tara Jane Goddard.

title: Overflowing Bins located at Whittington Castle
description:
Overflowing Bins located at Whittington Castle: Dear Shropshire Council, I am writing to report an ongoing issue with the public bins located at Whittington Castle.

The bins have been overflowing for well over a week now, and as of today, 5th May 2026, all three bins remain completely full, with rubbish spilling out onto the surrounding ground. The situation appears to be getting worse rather than improving.

This is not only unsightly but also poses a potential health and environmental risk. The accumulation of waste could attract rats and other vermin, increasing the likelihood of an infestation. This is particularly concerning given the presence of ducks in the area and the number of dogs regularly walked around the site, both of which could be affected.

I would appreciate it if this issue could be addressed as soon as possible, including clearing the current waste and reviewing the frequency of bin collections at this location to prevent recurrence or distributing more bins within the castle grounds to keep on top of the ongoing litter issue here and to do weekly checks or weekly bin collections.

Thank you for your attention to this matter.

Kind regards, Miss Emma Louise Finney.

I think the public would also benefit from a few more bins being distributed amongst the castle grounds, as I believe this would get on top of the ongoing litter issue. Also weekly bin collections would be good to stop problems arising and persisting.

Thank you for your attention to this matter.

Kind regards, Miss Emma Louise Finney.

Dear Shropshire Council, I am writing to report a concerning issue regarding the waste bins at Whittington Castle.

The bins at the site have been overflowing for well over a week, and as of today, 6th May 2026, all three bins remain completely full. Rubbish is spilling out onto the surrounding ground, and there is also a significant amount of dog mess left unattended in the area. This is not only unsightly but also poses a serious hygiene concern.

The current situation creates a real risk of attracting rats, which could lead to an infestation. This would have serious consequences for the local environment, including harm to ducks and other wildlife, as well as risks to dogs that are regularly walked in the area. Additionally, families, children, and babies frequently visit and walk around the castle grounds, and their health and safety could be impacted.

Furthermore, a potential rat infestation would be particularly damaging to the kitchen facilities at Whittington Castle, which have only recently reopened. Maintaining a clean and safe environment is essential for its continued operation.

To help address this issue, I would strongly suggest: ■ Increasing the number of waste bins around the castle grounds ■ Introducing more frequent (ideally weekly) waste collections ■ Ensuring regular monitoring and maintenance of the existing bins

These measures would greatly benefit the public and help preserve the cleanliness and safety of this well-loved local site.

I would appreciate it if this matter could be addressed as soon as possible.

Thank you for your attention.

Yours faithfully, Miss Tara Jane Goddard.

Request for Inspection and Management of Trees Adjacent to Residential Property
service_name: Tree problem on an open space
description:
Request for Inspection and Management of Trees Adjacent to Residential Property: Dear Sir/Madam,

I am writing to request that the council inspect several trees located on the public green space directly adjacent to my property at 4 Bryn Glas, Oswestry.

The trees include an English Oak, Norway Maple, European Beech, and Common Hawthorn. I have attached photographs showing their close proximity to neighbouring homes and boundary fencing.

Over recent years these trees have continued to increase in height and canopy spread, and I am becoming increasingly concerned about the potential risks they may pose if left unmanaged.

My concerns include:

• Branches and canopies extending very close to residential properties.

• The long-term suitability of large species such as the Oak, Norway Maple, and Beech in such close proximity to houses.

• Potential root impact on foundations, drainage, underground services, and nearby structures as the trees continue to mature.

• The risk of falling branches or structural failure during periods of severe weather or high winds.

• One tree appears to have a noticeable lean and may require professional assessment.

I appreciate the environmental value of trees; however, I believe these trees now require careful management to ensure they remain safe and appropriate for their location within a residential area.

I respectfully request that the council arrange a site inspection by a qualified arboricultural officer and consider any necessary remedial works, including pruning, crown reduction, ongoing maintenance, or removal where appropriate.

Please confirm that this matter has been logged and advise what action the council intends to take.

Yours faithfully, Elen Davies

Also I tried ringing but there was an answer machine message saying your IT systems are down so can't take calls.

Many thanks

description: Potholes at the junction of High fawr avenue with llanforda rise: High fawr avenue has had the road surface dressed in the last few days. There are still potholes (which filled with water during last night's rain) and the road surface is very uneven - especially at the junction with llanforda rise. In 2021 a motorcyclist had an accident as a result of this and had to take time off from running his own business as a consequence. We have waited a long time for the road improvements on high fawr avenue and it really seems as though the road surface hasn't been improved at all. It needs to be. Do you check the work that you commission? I recommend that you please come and inspect it for yourselves and see whether you think council funds have been well spent.

detail: The overgrowth on the Gobowen roundabout is stopping visibility for traffic, especially as we are an escape route from the bypass when there’s an accident and have large vehicles traversing the roundabout without any visibility . But I’m reporting the laurel trees which have become very invasive and I believe many councils have eradicated them ! This is my main complaint There are some beautiful silver birch trees on the roundabout and a multi stem Silverbirch which we planted as a memorial to our son who died in 2004, this is being overpowered by the laurels and has no space to breathe and flourish . The large wild roses are wonderful but not noticed . Work on the roundabout is well overdue and if it was extensively tackled I believe there is an initiative for the Derwen College students to manage it . Thank you for your consideration ,and hopefully soon to be action , to improve this space for reasons noted …. 🤗

Parish Council requests that litter bin is moved
detail: Selattyn and Gobowen Parish Council receives regular complaints from residents about the bin outside The Co-op, The Cross, Gobowen, SY11 3JR. I have attached a screenshot from Google Maps showing the bin in question. This bin is regularly overflowing and has refuse stacked up around it. The health hazard being caused by this situation outside the entrance to a food store should not be allowed to continue and the Parish Council requests that Shropshire Council move this bin away from the doors of the Co-op, to another location in the village centre. There is already inadequate bin provision / emptying for a village this size so the bin must be retained but in a different location. Parish Council representatives would be happy to meet a Shropshire Council officer to discuss suitable alternative locations in the village centre. Arrangements can be made by contacting the Parish Clerk.

title: Road works
description: Road works: The information regarding the road closure at Nort Drive just gives the dates 17/5/26 to 29/5/26. This road was only resurfaced last year, now back to Square 1 with an uneven surface. Some communication about which ends would be closed would have made the situation easier as the works changed closure by the weekend which we were never informed of to Agnes Hunt Drive. The diversion to Agnes Hunt Drive is miles out of the way. Why were the roadworks left in situ over the Bank Holiday when no one was working? We were in our motorhome trying to negotiate the narrow road with barriers down. The last two occasions I have been out two Lorries have blocked the road completely, no one around to explain. When I questioned the lorry driver he told me the road was closed. Today 7 cars left to wait while they used a grab lorry. No one there to direct or inform drivers.

I've reported this to other authorities and the wildlife trust, Shropshire star as newts are a protect species

I am working with a client that is blind that uses this pavement to collect a child from the primary school each day. Because the grasses are overgrown her guide dog is trying to take her around them (as they are an obstacle) which means both of them are walking on the edge of the pavement very close to the busy road. If it is possible to strim back the grasses / hedges along this section to make the pavement as wide as possible it would make it much safer for this client, the guide dog and the child to use. Thanks!

description:
Fatal Accident History & Road Safety Intervention Request: This section of road currently carries a default 60 mph National Speed Limit allowance, which ignores basic physics, the severe layout of the road, and a known history of severe and fatal accidents. This corner presents three compounding, critical hazards: 1. Extreme Braking Limitations: On this 17% descent, gravity heavily works against a vehicle’s brakes. According to Rule 126 of the Highway Code, stopping distances must be drastically increased on hills. When the road surface is wet, physics dictates that a vehicle traveling near the legal 60 mph threshold requires an estimated 241 metres to come to a stop—more than three times the standard baseline.

2. Terrain and the Sheer Drop: The physical geometry of this sweeping curve is completely blind on approach. Crucially, there is an unprotected sheer drop directly behind the perimeter of the corner. Any vehicle that fails to negotiate this turn due to excessive speed or a loss of traction faces a catastrophic fall, making any roadway departure inherently life-threatening.

3. Documented History of Serious and Fatal Crashes: This is not a theoretical hazard. This specific stretch of the B4580 past the Racecourse has seen recurring, severe incidents where vehicles have left the tarmac. This includes documented emergency turnouts involving cars landing upside down in fields, casualties requiring air ambulance extraction, and tragic fatalities. The status quo is actively costing lives.

Motorists simply cannot safely negotiate this sharp curve at the current legal speed threshold.

High Fawr Avenue - potholes and unsealed road surface
description: High Fawr Avenue - potholes and unsealed road surface: High Fawr Avenue was ''closed' for three days and supposedly treated. It DOES NOT have the same appearance as other roads which were treated at the same time - it appears that it was not sealed by contractors. The potholes in the road were not filled before new gravel was distributed, so the road surface is now worse than it was before remedial work was supposedly undertaken. This applies to the entire length of High Fawr Avenue. On returning home, the wheels of our car have gravel embedded in them. I telephoned the pothole hotline on May 18th to discuss the potholes and was told that 'perhaps' the contractors would fill the hoes before sealing. The potholes were not treated. Sealing has not taken place, as evidenced by the quantities of gravel on the road and the noise of gravel chippings when driving over the surface. I can only see this as a waste of public funds, as the potholes will continue to degrade in bad weather.

detail:
We are residents and homeowners at 43 Victoria Road, Oswestry, SY11 2HT.

There are several trees and hedges adjacent to our property that have become overgrown from the public footpath side (adjacent Butterfly Dental Practise). We would be grateful if the tree could be trimmed, as they are now encroaching into our garden. There is risk that the branches of the tree could fall and injure our fence/fall in our garden.

We also have a toddler, and we have noticed that the hedge contains sharp thorns and that nettles are growing in the area. These pose a risk of irritation and injury to both our family and members of the public walking past. The hedge was cut last year and the thorny hedge fell in our front yard and this thorn gave rise to a puncture to our car. Hence, once the hedge is cut; could these be cleared from our front yard and garden as well? The thorns pose a threat to our young son as well.

We would greatly appreciate it if you could arrange for the necessary maintenance to be carried out at your earliest convenience.

detail:
The Potholes are very dangerous as they are very deep and will cause further damage to the road surface.

They are so dangerous that they can cause a car at high speed to alter its direction of travel causing a fatality. This can cause a car to mount the pavement causing severe risk to the public.

Cae Glass Park runs along side the road and there are often infants and children crossing the road and it’s a main thoroughfare for secondary school children and the general public not to mention the people who live near there.

The Potholes are extremely dangerous, it’s been present since Feb/March 26 but has progressively gotten worse through time.

title: Over grown trees along Liverppol Road impacting on allotments
description:
Over grown trees along Liverppol Road impacting on allotments: Trees have not been maintained for many years...10 years. These now have a 15-yard overhang into the adjacent allotments on both sides of Liverpool Road, with roots stretching beyond. Allotments pre dated planting of these trees, that now impact ability to grow produce due to shade and lack of rain due to canopy shelter. Also branches now bend and are starting to hit sheds , greenhouses and toilet roofs.

Branches continually drop onto plots and roofs, its only a matter of time until damage will be beyond repair.

description: 3 months with no entry sign: I've reported an issue with these 'no-entry' signs for TWO years. The latest situation is that Shropshire Council cut the sign down three months ago, taped it up and left. We now have ONE no-entry sign, and cars, vans and lorries continue to enter the one way system. It is truly baffling what this isn't considered dangerous. Why would the council take it down and not have it replaced immediately??? Motorists and pedestrians alike are in daily peril with this. Please give this urgent attention.

Traffic Light Timing Issues: Traffic lights on the Upper Brook Street and Lower Brook Street side, the green lights are on for too short a time, Upper Church Street and Church Street side they are on for too long. I don't know if this is the case for all of the day but at 5:15 in the morning, you can barely get to the centre of the cross roads before they are turning red (5 seconds?) whereas Upper Church Street/Church street side is on for a least a minute, with no traffic going through them (maybe 1 car) meanwhile traffic has built up and if the car in front is slow to set off, you're back to a red light again.

Please, please, please, can this be looked in to, I know it sounds trivial to some people but when you're sat at a red light and there is no traffic to wait for, it just puts you in a bad mood for the rest of the day.

title: Church Street towards the Cross - water pools whenever it rains
description:
Church Street towards the Cross - water pools whenever it rains: Church Street towards the Cross - water pools in deep puddles whenever it rains.

The drainage grids running through the chicanes along the road are completely blocked. This prevents rainwater from running through them and going down the drain.

The disabled blue marked parking area gets completely flooded outside Boots, as does the pedestrian crossing outside Greggs. The water is over "shoe" depth.

In the photo you can see the water runs on the outside of the chicane, rather than flowing through the grid in the pavement.

The new road painting for the lanes on the ground (done today 10.07.2026) have been painted too narrow. Neither of the two lanes facing east of wide enough for a standard car. It looks like they painted the centre line straight down the middle rather than two thirds of the way across so the result is two ¼ lanes and one ½ lane rather than 3 ⅓. Its an accident waiting to happen. Its tight there as it is before it was resurfaced. If people are in the middle lane (turning right) no cars will be able to get through the left lane (straight on or turning left). Its a shame as this resurfacing is very valued and needed, can this be rectified while the painters are still out?

description: Relocation of street light: I am writing to enquire about the possibility of relocating a street lamp outside my property - 99 Maple Avenue, Oswestry, Shropshire SY11 2SF. I would like to widen my existing driveway to create additional off road parking, but the current position of the lamp post prevents this from being possible. Parking on our road has become increasingly difficult due to the number of vehicles. It is often extremely hard for visitors to find somewhere to park, and unfortunately this has contributed to tensions and neighbour disputes, which appear to be escalating. There have also been occasions where vehicles have been physically blocked in by other parked cars because of the lack of space. My intention is to widen the driveway sufficiently so that a visitors vehicle can park alongside my own, entirely within my frontage. This would reduce the need for visitors to park on the road and help avoid vehicles obstructing the dropped kerb or restricting access for others. I believe this would not only benefit my property but also help ease some of the parking pressures on the street. Could you please advise whether it would be possible to relocate the lamp post and explain the process involved? I would also appreciate information on whether a site visit would be required and an indication of the likely costs. If there are any application forms or further information you require from me, please let me know. I have also attached a photograph showing the current position of the lamp post and the existing driveway to help illustrate why relocating the column would allow the driveway to be widened. Thank you for your time and consideration. I look forward to hearing from you. Kind regards

Request for highway safety assessment
description:
Request for highway safety assessment: I would like to request that Shropshire Council carry out a highway safety assessment of the junction on Maesbury Road, Oswestry (What3Words: ///export.contour.gratitude), to determine whether improvements are needed.

This is a busy junction serving a business and industrial area. During busy periods, turning right out of the side road from Aico onto Maesbury Road can be difficult because of the volume of traffic.

The concern is that prolonged waits to find a safe gap may increase the likelihood of drivers accepting smaller gaps in traffic than they otherwise would, which has the potential to increase the risk of collisions. As traffic volumes continue to grow, I believe it would be appropriate for the junction to be reviewed to ensure it continues to operate safely.

I would be grateful if the Highways team could investigate whether any improvements to the junction would improve safety. This could include reviewing traffic movements, visibility, road markings, junction layout, or any other measures that the Council considers appropriate.

I am not requesting a specific engineering solution, but I would appreciate a professional assessment of the junction to determine whether any changes are justified.

Thank you for your time and consideration.
detail:

Thank you for your time and consideration.

title: Two potholes causing excessive Noise
description: Two potholes causing excessive Noise: The attached photo clearly shows two potholes adjacent to a raised pedestrian crossing. These potholes are causing significant noise from heavy lorries traveling to and from the waterworks, as well as from farmers towing trailers. In a previous report, you stated that repairs would be made in March of this year. To date, no action has been taken, and the noise pollution has worsened. Additionally, I previously reported that traffic regularly exceeds the 20-mph speed limit in this area to outrun vehicles coming down the hill from both directions. This speeding occurs across two pedestrian crossings used by schools and local residents, creating a serious safety hazard.
